title: Conversational Query Rewriting
concept: Resolving pronouns and follow-up context ('what about pricing?') into a standalone, retrievable query.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about Query Expansion with Synonyms. What is its main purpose? is covered Query Expansion with Synonyms. What is with Synonyms. What is the primary purpose of expanding a query with synonyms?

options.text: Adding related terms to widen the search net.
Summarizing long user queries into a single keyword.
Translating search terms into other languages.

exercises.explain: Synonym expansion adds related words (like 'sofa' for 'couch') to ensure we don't miss relevant documents that use different vocabulary.

cards.title: The Follow-up Problem
cards.body: Chatbots are great at remembering conversations, but database search engines are not! When a user asks a follow-up question like 'how about pricing?', standard search engines fail because they don't know what 'it' refers to. We need to bridge this gap.

cards.title: Conversational Query Rewriting
cards.body: Conversational Query Rewriting is the process of taking a user's short, contextual follow-up message and rewriting it into a fully detailed, standalone search query using the previous chat history.

cards.title: The Mental Translator
cards.body: Imagine you are a busy assistant listening to a conversation. If someone says 'How is the weather in Paris?' and then follows up with 'And what about London?', you mentally translate the second half into 'How is the weather in London?' before looking it up.

cards.title: Context in Action
cards.body: A user asks: 'Who founded Microsoft?' followed by: 'When was it started?'. The rewriter transforms the second query into: 'When was Microsoft started?' so the search engine can find the correct answer.

cards.title: Don't Dump the Whole Chat
cards.body: People think chatbots automatically send the entire chat history to the search engine. In reality, sending a whole conversation transcript creates messy search results; rewriting the final question into one clean search query works much better.

cards.title: Before vs. After Rewriting
cards.body: See how a conversational follow-up is transformed into a clean, searchable query by merging it with the conversation history.

left.text: 'Where can I buy one?' (Referencing 'smartwatch' from earlier)

exercises.prompt: What does 'resolving pronouns' mean in the context of query rewriting?

options.text: Deleting pronouns from the query completely.
Replacing vague pronouns with the actual nouns they refer to from earlier in the chat.
Splitting the query into multiple synonyms.

exercises.explain: Pronouns like 'it', 'they', or 'he' refer to things mentioned earlier. Resolving them means replacing them with the actual names or nouns from the chat history.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a user asks 'how about pricing?' as a follow-up, a standard vector database will search for the correct product automatically without any rewriting.
exercises.explain: Without rewriting, the search engine only receives the literal text 'how about pricing?' without knowing the topic, which usually leads to irrelevant or missing search results.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe the goal of rewriting conversational queries.
exercises.sentence: The goal of conversational query rewriting is to turn a vague follow-up into a standalone query.
exercises.explain: We rewrite conversational follow-ups into standalone queries so that our search database (which has no memory of the conversation) can retrieve the correct documents using only the rewritten text.

exercises.prompt: A user asks 'Do you sell running shoes?' followed by 'Are they waterproof?'. Which of the following is the AI-rewritten standalone query?
exercises.explain: Option C is a perfect standalone query that resolves 'they' into 'running shoes' and 'waterproof' into 'are they waterproof', making it searchable without needing the chat history. Options A and B are conversational and incomplete.

exercises.prompt: Your chatbot is helping a user research the 'Enterprise Plan'. The user's last message is: 'And how much does it cost?'. How does a Conversational Query Rewriter handle this to ensure a successful database search?

options.text: It translates the query into multiple foreign languages to search global databases.
It fuses the chat history and the current vague question into a complete, standalone search query.
It filters out all pronouns from the database index so they are never searched.
It forces the user to retype their entire question with complete nouns.

exercises.explain: Conversational Query Rewriting translates 'And how much does it cost?' into a standalone query like 'Pricing details for the Enterprise Plan' by incorporating the chat history. Without this step, the database search engine receives a useless query ('how much does it cost?') that lacks the context of 'Enterprise Plan'.
